steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student predicts that birds with a mean body mass greater than 150 grams do not use broken-wing display. To weaken this prediction, you need table data showing a bird heavier than 150 grams that does perform broken-wing display — a direct counterexample.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Swamp palm bulbul uses broken-wing display; blue-winged teal does not': This says the teal does not use broken-wing display, which would actually support the prediction, not weaken it. The claim also conflicts with the table data shown. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Ruddy turnstone uses broken-wing display and is larger than the swamp palm bulbul': This shows the turnstone is larger than the bulbul, but the ruddy turnstone's mass is well under 150 grams. Both birds are small, so the comparison never crosses the 150-gram threshold and does not challenge the prediction. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Ruddy turnstone and swamp palm bulbul both under 150 grams and use broken-wing display': Both species are below the threshold. This is consistent with the prediction that small birds use broken-wing display and therefore supports rather than weakens it. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Blue-winged teal has mean body mass of 398 grams and performs broken-wing display': The blue-winged teal is well above 150 grams and still uses broken-wing display. This is a direct counterexample to the prediction, which claimed large birds do not use deceptive defenses. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
